---
title: "What Does Area Have to Do with Slope?"
subtitle: "Chapter 9"
date: 2023-04-01
---

This lesson explores a deeper perspective on why integration and differentiation
are inverse operations. By examining the problem of computing the average value
of a continuous function — specifically $\sin(x)$ on $[0, \pi]$ — we discover
that the average value of a function equals the slope of its antiderivative
between the endpoints of the interval. This reframing connects the notion of
area under a curve to the notion of slope of another curve.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Prerequisites

- Understanding of the definite integral as a limit of Riemann sums (Chapters 1 and 2)
- Familiarity with derivatives and antiderivatives (Chapters 3 and 7)
- The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Chapter 8)
- Knowledge of basic trigonometric functions and their derivatives
:::

## Topics Covered

- The average value of a continuous function as an integral
- Computing $\int_0^\pi \sin(x)\, dx$ via the antiderivative $-\cos(x)$
- Reinterpreting the average value as the slope of the antiderivative graph
- Why the average slope of tangent lines equals the overall slope between endpoints
- A second intuition for the inverse relationship between integrals and derivatives

## Lecture Video

```{=html}
<video controls width="100%" preload="metadata">
  <source src="https://github.com/ymote/3b1b-calculus/releases/download/v1.0/09_What%20does%20area%20have%20to%20do%20with%20slope%EF%BC%9F%20%EF%BD%9C%20Chapter%209%2C%20Essence%20of%20calculus.mp4" type="video/mp4">
</video>
```

## Key Video Frames

![The graph of $\sin(x)$ on $[0, \pi]$ and the question of its average height](../images/ch09/frame_01.jpg)

![Approximating the average with a finite sample of heights](../images/ch09/frame_02.jpg)

![The antiderivative $-\cos(x)$ and its slope between endpoints](../images/ch09/frame_03.jpg)

![The average value of $f$ as the slope of the antiderivative graph between $a$ and $b$](../images/ch09/frame_04.jpg)

## Key Concepts

### The Average of a Continuous Variable

We begin with a deceptively simple question: what is the average height of
$\sin(x)$ on the interval $[0, \pi]$? For a finite collection of numbers, the
average is computed by summing the values and dividing by their count. However,
a continuous function assumes infinitely many values, and we cannot simply "add
them all up and divide by infinity."

The resolution is to approximate. We sample $n$ points evenly spaced by
$dx = \pi / n$ across $[0, \pi]$. The average of these sampled heights is

$$
\text{Average} \approx \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sin(x_i).
$$

Since $n = \pi / dx$, we may rewrite this as

$$
\text{Average} \approx \frac{\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sin(x_i) \, dx}{\pi}.
$$

The numerator is precisely a Riemann sum. As $dx \to 0$, it converges to the
definite integral, giving us the fundamental formula for the average value of a
continuous function:

$$
\text{Average value of } f \text{ on } [a, b] = \frac{1}{b - a} \int_a^b f(x)\, dx.
$$

In words, the average value is the area under the graph divided by the width
of the interval.

### Evaluating the Integral of Sine

To compute $\int_0^\pi \sin(x)\, dx$, we require an antiderivative of $\sin(x)$.
Since $\frac{d}{dx}[-\cos(x)] = \sin(x)$, the antiderivative is $F(x) = -\cos(x)$.
Applying the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus:

$$
\int_0^\pi \sin(x)\, dx = F(\pi) - F(0) = [-\cos(\pi)] - [-\cos(0)] = 1 - (-1) = 2.
$$

Thus the area under one arch of the sine curve is exactly $2$. The average
height is therefore

$$
\text{Average} = \frac{2}{\pi} \approx 0.6366.
$$

### Interactive Desmos Graph: Average Value of Sine

```{=html}
<div id="calc_ch09_1" class="desmos-container"></div>
<script src="https://www.desmos.com/api/v1.9/calculator.js?apiKey=dcb31709b452b1cf9dc26972add0fda6"></script>
<script>
  var calc_ch09_1 = Desmos.GraphingCalculator(document.getElementById('calc_ch09_1'), {
    expressions: true, settingsMenu: false, xAxisLabel: 'x', yAxisLabel: 'y'
  });
  calc_ch09_1.setExpression({ id: 'sinx', latex: 'y = \\sin(x) \\left\\{0 \\le x \\le \\pi\\right\\}', color: '#2d70b3' });
  calc_ch09_1.setExpression({ id: 'n', latex: 'n = 10', sliderBounds: { min: 2, max: 80, step: 1 } });
  calc_ch09_1.setExpression({ id: 'dx', latex: 'd = \\frac{\\pi}{n}' });
  calc_ch09_1.setExpression({ id: 'rects', latex: '0 \\le y \\le \\sin\\left(\\operatorname{floor}\\left(\\frac{x}{d}\\right) \\cdot d + \\frac{d}{2}\\right) \\left\\{0 \\le x \\le \\pi\\right\\}', color: '#388c46', fillOpacity: 0.3 });
  calc_ch09_1.setExpression({ id: 'avg_line', latex: 'y = \\frac{2}{\\pi} \\left\\{0 \\le x \\le \\pi\\right\\}', color: '#c74440', lineStyle: Desmos.Styles.DASHED });
  calc_ch09_1.setExpression({ id: 'avg_label', latex: '\\frac{2}{\\pi}', color: '#c74440' });
  calc_ch09_1.setMathBounds({ left: -0.5, right: 4, bottom: -0.3, top: 1.5 });
</script>
```

Adjust the slider $n$ to increase the number of sample points. The green
rectangles illustrate the Riemann sum approximation. The dashed red line at
height $2/\pi \approx 0.6366$ marks the exact average value. As $n$ grows, the
sum of rectangle areas divided by $\pi$ converges to this value.

### The Average Value as the Slope of the Antiderivative

Here lies the central insight of this chapter. The average value we computed,

$$
\frac{1}{\pi - 0}\int_0^\pi \sin(x)\, dx = \frac{F(\pi) - F(0)}{\pi - 0},
$$

is nothing other than the **slope of the secant line** of the antiderivative
$F(x) = -\cos(x)$ between the points $(0, F(0))$ and $(\pi, F(\pi))$.

More generally, for any continuous function $f$ on $[a, b]$ with antiderivative
$F$, the average value formula becomes

$$
\frac{1}{b - a}\int_a^b f(x)\, dx = \frac{F(b) - F(a)}{b - a}.
$$

The left-hand side is the average value of $f$. The right-hand side is the
slope of the antiderivative graph between the endpoints. These are the same
quantity.

### Why Average Slope Equals Overall Slope

Since $f(x) = F'(x)$, the value of $f$ at each point $x$ is the slope of the
tangent line to the antiderivative graph at that point. Therefore, the average
value of $f$ over $[a, b]$ is the **average of all the tangent-line slopes**
of $F$ on that interval.

It is geometrically natural that the average of all instantaneous slopes of a
curve over an interval should equal the overall slope between the two endpoints.
If the curve rises by a total amount $\Delta F = F(b) - F(a)$ over a horizontal
span of $\Delta x = b - a$, then no matter how the curve oscillates in between,
the average rate of ascent must be $\Delta F / \Delta x$.

This provides a second, complementary intuition for why integration and
differentiation are inverses: the integral of $f$ (area under the curve)
divided by the interval length equals the slope of the antiderivative
(a derivative-like quantity), precisely because $f$ itself is the derivative
of that antiderivative.

### Interactive Desmos Graph: Antiderivative Slope

```{=html}
<div id="calc_ch09_2" class="desmos-container"></div>
<script>
  var calc_ch09_2 = Desmos.GraphingCalculator(document.getElementById('calc_ch09_2'), {
    expressions: true, settingsMenu: false, xAxisLabel: 'x', yAxisLabel: 'y'
  });
  calc_ch09_2.setExpression({ id: 'F', latex: 'F(x) = -\\cos(x)', color: '#388c46' });
  calc_ch09_2.setExpression({ id: 'a', latex: 'a = 0', sliderBounds: { min: 0, max: 3, step: 0.01 } });
  calc_ch09_2.setExpression({ id: 'b', latex: 'b = \\pi', sliderBounds: { min: 0.5, max: 6.28, step: 0.01 } });
  calc_ch09_2.setExpression({ id: 'ptA', latex: '(a, -\\cos(a))', color: '#c74440', pointStyle: Desmos.Styles.POINT, pointSize: 9 });
  calc_ch09_2.setExpression({ id: 'ptB', latex: '(b, -\\cos(b))', color: '#c74440', pointStyle: Desmos.Styles.POINT, pointSize: 9 });
  calc_ch09_2.setExpression({ id: 'secant', latex: 'y = -\\cos(a) + \\frac{-\\cos(b) + \\cos(a)}{b - a}(x - a)', color: '#c74440', lineStyle: Desmos.Styles.DASHED });
  calc_ch09_2.setMathBounds({ left: -0.5, right: 7, bottom: -1.5, top: 1.5 });
</script>
```

The green curve is the antiderivative $F(x) = -\cos(x)$. The dashed red line
is the secant connecting $(a, F(a))$ and $(b, F(b))$. Adjust the sliders $a$
and $b$ to observe how the slope of this secant always equals the average value
of $\sin(x)$ on $[a, b]$.

### Interactive Desmos Graph: Generalising to Any Function

```{=html}
<div id="calc_ch09_3" class="desmos-container"></div>
<script>
  var calc_ch09_3 = Desmos.GraphingCalculator(document.getElementById('calc_ch09_3'), {
    expressions: true, settingsMenu: false, xAxisLabel: 'x', yAxisLabel: 'y'
  });
  calc_ch09_3.setExpression({ id: 'f', latex: 'f(x) = x^2', color: '#2d70b3' });
  calc_ch09_3.setExpression({ id: 'F', latex: 'F(x) = \\frac{x^3}{3}', color: '#388c46' });
  calc_ch09_3.setExpression({ id: 'a', latex: 'a = 0', sliderBounds: { min: -2, max: 3, step: 0.1 } });
  calc_ch09_3.setExpression({ id: 'b', latex: 'b = 3', sliderBounds: { min: 0.5, max: 5, step: 0.1 } });
  calc_ch09_3.setExpression({ id: 'shade', latex: '0 \\le y \\le x^2 \\left\\{a \\le x \\le b\\right\\}', color: '#2d70b3', fillOpacity: 0.2 });
  calc_ch09_3.setExpression({ id: 'avg_line', latex: 'y = \\frac{F(b) - F(a)}{b - a} \\left\\{a \\le x \\le b\\right\\}', color: '#c74440', lineStyle: Desmos.Styles.DASHED });
  calc_ch09_3.setMathBounds({ left: -1, right: 6, bottom: -1, top: 12 });
</script>
```

Here $f(x) = x^2$ (blue) with its antiderivative $F(x) = x^3/3$ (green). The
shaded region shows the area under $f$ on $[a, b]$. The dashed red line
indicates the average value $\frac{F(b) - F(a)}{b - a}$. Adjust $a$ and $b$
to verify that the area divided by the interval width always agrees with the
secant slope of the antiderivative.

### Generalising the Finite Average

It is instructive to revisit the passage from finite to continuous more
carefully. Given $n$ sample points $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ spaced by
$dx = (b - a)/n$, the finite average is

$$
\bar{f}_n = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} f(x_i) = \frac{1}{b - a} \sum_{i=1}^{n} f(x_i)\, dx.
$$

The second equality follows from substituting $n = (b - a)/dx$. As $n \to \infty$
(equivalently $dx \to 0$), the Riemann sum in the numerator converges to
$\int_a^b f(x)\, dx$, yielding

$$
\bar{f} = \frac{1}{b - a}\int_a^b f(x)\, dx.
$$

This derivation highlights a recurring theme in calculus: whenever one wishes
to generalise a discrete operation (here, averaging finitely many values) to a
continuous setting, the natural tool is the integral.

### Interactive Python: Average Value Approximation

```{=html}
<div class="pyodide-container">
  <textarea class="code-input" id="code_ch09_1">
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Approximate the average value of sin(x) on [0, pi]
a, b = 0, np.pi
N_values = [5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 500]
exact_avg = 2 / np.pi

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(12, 7))
axes = axes.flatten()

for idx, N in enumerate(N_values):
    dx = (b - a) / N
    x_samples = np.linspace(a + dx/2, b - dx/2, N)
    heights = np.sin(x_samples)
    approx_avg = np.mean(heights)

    x_fine = np.linspace(a, b, 500)
    axes[idx].plot(x_fine, np.sin(x_fine), 'b-', linewidth=1.5)
    axes[idx].bar(x_samples, heights, width=dx*0.9, alpha=0.4,
                  color='green', edgecolor='green')
    axes[idx].axhline(y=exact_avg, color='red', linestyle='--',
                      linewidth=1, label=f'Exact = {exact_avg:.4f}')
    axes[idx].axhline(y=approx_avg, color='orange', linestyle=':',
                      linewidth=1, label=f'Approx = {approx_avg:.4f}')
    axes[idx].set_title(f'N = {N}')
    axes[idx].set_xlim(-0.2, 3.5)
    axes[idx].set_ylim(-0.1, 1.2)
    axes[idx].legend(fontsize=7)

plt.suptitle('Average value of sin(x) on [0, pi]', fontsize=13)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
  </textarea>
  <button class="run-btn" onclick="runPyodide('code_ch09_1', 'output_ch09_1', 'plot_ch09_1')">Run ▶</button>
  <div class="output" id="output_ch09_1"></div>
  <div class="plot-output" id="plot_ch09_1"></div>
</div>
```

### Interactive Python: Antiderivative Slope Equals Average Value

```{=html}
<div class="pyodide-container">
  <textarea class="code-input" id="code_ch09_2">
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Demonstrate: slope of antiderivative = average value of function
x = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 500)

# f(x) = sin(x), F(x) = -cos(x)
f = np.sin(x)
F = -np.cos(x)

a, b = 0.5, 4.0  # interval endpoints
avg_value = (-np.cos(b) - (-np.cos(a))) / (b - a)
secant_slope = ((-np.cos(b)) - (-np.cos(a))) / (b - a)

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))

# Left panel: f(x) = sin(x) with shaded area
ax1.plot(x, f, 'b-', linewidth=1.5, label='$f(x) = \\sin(x)$')
mask = (x >= a) & (x <= b)
ax1.fill_between(x[mask], f[mask], alpha=0.3, color='blue')
ax1.axhline(y=avg_value, color='red', linestyle='--',
            label=f'Avg value = {avg_value:.4f}')
ax1.axhline(y=0, color='gray', linewidth=0.5)
ax1.set_xlabel('x')
ax1.set_title('$f(x) = \\sin(x)$ and its average on $[a, b]$')
ax1.legend()

# Right panel: F(x) = -cos(x) with secant line
ax2.plot(x, F, 'g-', linewidth=1.5, label='$F(x) = -\\cos(x)$')
ax2.plot([a, b], [-np.cos(a), -np.cos(b)], 'ro-', markersize=8)
secant_line = -np.cos(a) + secant_slope * (x - a)
ax2.plot(x, secant_line, 'r--', linewidth=1,
         label=f'Secant slope = {secant_slope:.4f}')
ax2.axhline(y=0, color='gray', linewidth=0.5)
ax2.set_xlabel('x')
ax2.set_title('$F(x) = -\\cos(x)$ and its secant on $[a, b]$')
ax2.legend()

plt.suptitle(
    f'Average value = {avg_value:.4f},  '
    f'Secant slope = {secant_slope:.4f}  (they match)',
    fontsize=12, fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
  </textarea>
  <button class="run-btn" onclick="runPyodide('code_ch09_2', 'output_ch09_2', 'plot_ch09_2')">Run ▶</button>
  <div class="output" id="output_ch09_2"></div>
  <div class="plot-output" id="plot_ch09_2"></div>
</div>
```

## Summary

::: {.key-formula}
| Concept | Key Result |
|---|---|
| Average value of $f$ on $[a, b]$ | $\displaystyle \bar{f} = \frac{1}{b-a}\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ |
| Average of $\sin(x)$ on $[0, \pi]$ | $\displaystyle \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^\pi \sin(x)\,dx = \frac{2}{\pi} \approx 0.6366$ |
| Average as antiderivative slope | $\displaystyle \frac{1}{b-a}\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = \frac{F(b) - F(a)}{b - a}$ |
| Why area relates to slope | $f(x) = F'(x)$, so the average of $f$ (area$\div$width) equals the average slope of $F$ (rise$\div$run) |
:::
